channel.title: NYT > World > Middle East
channel.copyright: Copyright 2025 The New York Times Company

item.title: Israeli Strikes on Gaza Kills Over 400, in Breakdown of Cease-Fire
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s office said he had ordered the aerial attacks after Hamas’s “repeated refusal” to release the remaining hostages it holds. “This is just the beginning,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Patrick Kingsley, Rawan Sheikh Ahmad, Yan Zhuang, Aaron Boxerman and Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Palestinians at a hospital in Gaza City on Tuesday with the body of someone killed in Israeli airstrikes.

item.title: Hamas Does Not Respond Militarily to Renewed Israeli Attack on Gaza
item.description: “Hamas doesn’t want an escalation, but it will not surrender,” said one Palestinian analyst close to the militant group, which has been weakened by a 15-month war with Israel.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hamas fighters during a handover of Israeli hostages last month.

item.title: Why Did Israel Resume Airstrikes on Gaza? What to Know About the Attacks
item.description: The deadly airstrikes shattered a period of relative calm and raised the prospect of a return to all-out war.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Praying over the bodies of Palestinians killed in Gaza City on Tuesday.

item.title: Israel Resumes Attacks in Gaza After Stalled Cease-Fire Talks with Hamas
item.description: While the Israeli government has stopped short of a ground invasion for now, its new strikes in Gaza seem to be an effort to force concessions from Hamas.
item.dc:creator: Patrick Kingsley and Ronen Bergman
item.media:description: An injured man searching through the rubble of his home in Nuseirat, in central Gaza, after an Israeli strike on Tuesday.

item.title: Fewer Than Half of the 59 Hostages in Gaza Believed to Be Alive
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Tuesday’s attack on Gaza came after Hamas said no to the latest proposals by mediators, which included releasing the remaining captives.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Einav Zangauker, center, the mother of Matan Zangauker, an Israeli hostage held in Gaza, near the Gaza border in Kibbutz Nir Oz, Israel, on Tuesday.

item.title: Netanyahu’s Move to Fire Shin Bet Chief Reflects Wider Push for Control
item.description: The effort is part of a dispute between Benjamin Netanyahu’s right-wing alliance and its opponents about the nature and future of the Israeli state.
item.dc:creator: Patrick Kingsley
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s effort to remove Israel’s domestic intelligence is raising concerns about whether he was seeking to undermine the agency’s independence.

item.title: Violence in Syria Shows Difficulty in Unifying Armed Forces
item.description: Sectarian-driven killings of civilians this month displayed the government’s weak control over both its own forces and affiliated fighters, experts said.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: A spasm of violence erupted in Syria this month, pitting the new government’s security forces, and fighters affiliated with it, against suspected remnants of the old regime.

item.title: Netanyahu Moves to Fire Israel’s Domestic Intelligence Chief
item.description: The Israeli prime minister’s effort to remove the Shin Bet chief is raising concerns about whether he was seeking to undermine the agency’s independence.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon and Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: Ronen Bar, the Shin Bet director, at a memorial in Jerusalem in October.

item.title: Houthis Vow Retaliation After U.S. Strikes in Yemen
item.description: The Iranian-backed rebels, who have targeted Israel and shipping in the Red Sea, said children were among those killed in the strikes ordered by President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Ismaeel Naar and Saeed Al-Batati
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A damaged building in Saada, Yemen, on Sunday after U.S. strikes.

item.title: Israel’s Newest Army Recruits: the Ultra-Orthodox
item.description: Israel’s Newest Army Recruits: the Ultra-Orthodox
item.dc:creator: Sergey Ponomarev, Jack Nicas and Adam Sella
item.media:description: Israel’s ultra-Orthodox Jews were once exempt from military service — but now they are being drafted.

item.title: Israel Keeps Up Attacks in Gaza Despite Truce
item.description: In the latest strikes, the Israeli military killed at least nine people, according to the Gaza health ministry. Israel says militants were threatening its forces.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.media:description: Mourners weep next to the bodies of Palestinians killed in an Israeli strike in northern Gaza on Saturday.

item.title: Syrian Druse Pilgrims Pay Rare Visit to Israel
item.description: As part of an effort to deepen its influence in southern Syria, Israel has been seeking stronger ties with the Druse religious minority that holds sway there.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman

item.title: Bread Lines and Salty Drinking Water: Israeli Aid Block Sets Gaza Back Again
item.description: Shipments surged into Gaza after Israel and Hamas reached a cease-fire, even if they weren’t enough. Then Israel blocked the border again to pressure Hamas in truce talks.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Yee, Bilal Shbair and Saher Alghorra
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Food handouts in Beit Lahia this week.

item.title: Senior Islamic State Leader Killed in Joint Iraqi-U.S. Operation, Iraq Says
item.description: The killing comes as the group has been reconstituting in Syria, carrying out more attacks than at any time since it lost control of its territory nearly six years ago.
item.dc:creator: Alissa J. Rubin
item.category: Islamic State in Iraq and Syria (ISIS)
item.media:description: Iraqi Prime Minister Mohammed Shia al-Sudani, seen here at the Camp Taji military base in Iraq in January, called the Islamic State leader who was killed “one of the most dangerous terrorists in the world.”

item.title: Hamas Says It’s Willing to Release U.S.-Israeli Hostage Edan Alexander
item.description: Hamas said it agreed to release Edan Alexander and turn over the bodies of four other hostages. But both the White House and Israel said Hamas wasn’t serious, suggesting a deal wasn’t close.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Adam Rasgon
item.media:description: Yael Alexander holding a poster of her son, Edan, at a rally in Tel Aviv last month.

item.title: China Backs Iran in Nuclear Talks, Slams ‘Threat of Force’ From the West
item.description: Beijing sought to position itself as a key player in negotiations with Iran over its nuclear program, as President Trump called for talks with Tehran.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher and Berry Wang
item.media:description: China hosted talks in Beijing on Friday with the deputy foreign ministers of Russia and Iran to discuss Iran’s nuclear program. China said sanctions on Iran should be lifted.

item.title: ‘Song of the North’ Uses Puppets to Help a Persian Epic Spring to Life
item.description: Hamid Rahmanian has made it his life’s work to share the richness of Iranian culture. “Song of the North,” at the New Victory Theater, is just the latest installment.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler and Gavin Doran
item.media:credit: Gavin Doran for The New York Times

item.title: U.N. Accuses Israel of Targeting Reproductive Health Facilities in Gaza
item.description: A report by a U.N. committee found that Israel has committed “genocidal acts” intended to prevent births. Israel quickly rejected the findings as biased.
item.dc:creator: Nick Cumming-Bruce
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Al Basma I.V.F. Center, Gaza’s largest fertility clinic, was struck by an Israeli shell during the war with Hamas.

item.title: Bardella, Leader of France’s Far-Right National Rally, Heads to Israel
item.description: As Jordan Bardella, its young president, tries to distance the party from its history of antisemitism, it is making common cause with Israel against “Islamist ideology.”
item.dc:creator: Roger Cohen
item.media:description: Jordan Bardella, president of the National Rally party, has campaigned against Islamist movements and immigration, and made common cause with Israel.

item.title: Houthi Drones Could Become Stealthier and Fly Farther
item.description: A new report documented efforts to smuggle hydrogen fuel cell components into Yemen that will provide Houthi fighters a technological leap ahead.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.media:description: Evidence examined by weapons researchers shows that Houthi rebels may have acquired technology that makes drones more difficult to detect.

item.title: Israel-Hamas Talks Deadlocked as Trump Envoy Turns to Ukraine
item.description: The two sides are supposed to negotiate a second phase of the cease-fire agreement that would end the war, but they remain far apart on how to move forward.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: A soup kitchen in Beit Lahiya in northern Gaza this week.

item.title: After al-Assad’s Ouster, Many Syrians Struggle With Returning Home
item.description: Syria’s interim president has said that millions would return after President Bashar al-Assad’s ouster, but many houses and other buildings were destroyed in 13 years of civil war.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim and Laura Boushnak
item.media:credit: Laura Boushnak for The New York Times

item.title: Pokémon Go Maker Niantic Sells Unit to Saudi Fund for $3.5 Billion
item.description: Scopely said it would buy Niantic’s video game business, which includes Pokémon Go, for $3.5 billion. Saudi Arabia’s sovereign wealth fund acquired Scopely in 2023.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.media:description: The Pokémon Go Festival in New York in 2024.

item.title: Gabbard Drops Pick for Top Intel Post, a Critic of Israel on Gaza
item.description: The scuttled appointment highlighted an emerging foreign policy fault line in the new Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:description: Tulsi Gabbard, the director of national intelligence, had considered the appointment of an official sharing her skepticism of American intervention abroad.

item.title: Rubio Says a Cease-Fire in Ukraine Could Happen in ‘Days’ if Russia Agrees
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio said top diplomats from the Group of 7 allies meeting in Canada should focus on ending the war. And he shrugged off President Trump’s threats to annex Canada.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:description: “Here’s what we’d like the world to look like in a few days,” Secretary of State Marco Rubio said in Ireland on Wednesday. “Neither side is shooting at each other — not rockets, not missiles, not bullets, nothing, not artillery.”

item.title: Kurdish Fighters Called a Truce, but Turkey Kept Up Lethal Strikes
item.description: Turkey is still bombing armed Kurdish insurgents in Iraq and Syria, even after their leader urged them to lay down their arms and disband, and their group declared a cease-fire.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard and Safak Timur
item.media:description: A crowd in northeastern Syria watches the release of a statement by Abdullah Ocalan, the jailed leader of the Kurdish insurgent group P.K.K. in Turkey, last month.
